name: differentiated questions
top_issues: Wayground value prop is too vague to score above a 2: 'Wayground lets you build adaptable, standards-aligned question sets with diverse formats and instant insights' names no specific feature and could describe any edtech tool. A teacher reading this has no idea what to click or how Wayground actually saves them time on differentiated questioning. Needs a specific feature callout, e.g., 'Wayground's Create with AI lets you generate tiered question ladders for a single standard in under 3 minutes.'

Grade-band specificity is thin outside of the one Grade 7 science example. The Bloom/DOK table uses states-of-matter and freezing-point questions (middle school chemistry) throughout, but a 3rd-grade ELA teacher or a 10th-grade history teacher has nothing to anchor to. Adding even one non-science, non-middle-school example would push this from a 3 to a 4.
positive_signals: The Ask → Wait → Support → Probe → Lift table is genuinely classroom-ready — a teacher could laminate it and keep it on a podium. The combination of 'What You Do / What Students Do / Quick Supports' columns is exactly the kind of scannable reference busy teachers need during a planning period.

Term definitions are handled exceptionally well throughout: Bloom's, DOK, WIDA, wait time, and revoicing are all defined inline with no assumed expertise, and the explicit note that 'Bloom and DOK are not a one-to-one match' shows real pedagogical honesty that teachers will trust.
narrative: This article is substantively strong — the routine table, Bloom/DOK ladder, and tiered sentence stems are genuinely print-and-use quality, and the consistent inline definitions make it accessible to novice and veteran teachers alike. The single criterion that triggers a REVISE verdict is the Wayground call-to-action: "lets you build adaptable, standards-aligned question sets with diverse formats and instant insights" is a marketing placeholder, not a feature description, and a skeptical teacher will ignore it entirely. Before publish, replace it with the name of a specific Wayground feature and a concrete time-or-effort claim (e.g., "Wayground's Create with AI generates a ready-to-use tiered question ladder for any standard in about three minutes"). A secondary fix worth making: add one example outside middle school science — even a single "5th-grade ELA" or "9th-grade history" question ladder entry — so the article signals relevance to the full K–12 audience it claims to serve.
